title: The First Sunday after Christmas
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: December 29, 2024

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon falls on the First Sunday after Christmas Day, also known as the Feast of the Holy Family in some traditions. According to the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings would likely include 1 Samuel 2:18-26 or Isaiah 61:10-62:3, Luke 2:41-52 (the finding of Jesus in the temple), and Psalm 111 or 148. The church calendar places this in the Christmas Season, which extends from December 25 through Epiphany (January 6), a liturgical period focused on the incarnation and its implications.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

In late December 2024, the Episcopal Church was in the immediate aftermath of General Convention 2024 (held in Louisville in June), with dioceses implementing resolutions regarding inclusion, governance, and ecumenical relationships. The Diocese of Colorado, which encompasses Saint John's Cathedral, was navigating post-Convention implementation while preparing for the new church year. No major bishop elections or controversies specific to Colorado were in headlines at this exact date, though the broader church remained engaged in ongoing discussions about LGBTQ+ inclusion and Anglican Communion relationships.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver experienced typical late December weather with moderate temperatures in the 40s-50s Fahrenheit. The week before December 29 saw the city concluding its holiday season celebrations with New Year's Eve preparations underway. No major local crises or emergencies dominated Colorado headlines immediately before this sermon date; the region was in a typical post-Christmas news lull with focus on year-end reviews and New Year's planning.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The week ending December 29, 2024 was dominated by: (1) The aftermath of the New Orleans terrorist attack and Las Vegas explosion on January 1st being anticipated/discussed in forward-looking coverage, (2) Year-end economic reports showing inflation management and job market conditions, and (3) Political transition coverage with the incoming Trump administration scheduled to take office January 20, 2025. Congress was in recess following contentious year-end negotiations over the government budget and spending bills. Holiday-related cultural and travel stories filled much of the remaining news cycle.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Gaza humanitarian crisis remained acute, with ceasefire negotiations ongoing and widespread reports of displacement and suffering continuing through the holiday period. Russia's invasion of Ukraine maintained its grinding winter stalemate with no major breakthrough moments in late December. Middle Eastern tensions remained elevated across multiple theaters as the year concluded.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The nation was in the liminal space between Christmas celebration and New Year's resolution-making, with a sense of transition and uncertainty about the incoming political administration. Sports enthusiasts were focused on college football bowl season and the upcoming NFL playoffs. The cultural mood blended holiday exhaustion with anticipatory anxiety about 2025, while many Americans took advantage of the post-Christmas holiday period for rest and family time. Denver's congregation would likely have reflected both the peaceful family-focused themes of the Christmas liturgy and underlying concerns about the year ahead.
